title: Cat Coughing vs. Hairballs: Understanding the Difference and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat coughing or hacking up a hairball? Learn the symptoms of feline asthma and when to seek veterinary care for respiratory issues.
content_html:
What is it?

Many cat owners mistake coughing for the attempt to pass a hairball. When a cat hunches their back, extends their neck, and produces a dry hacking sound, they are often experiencing a true cough. While hairballs result in the physical expulsion of fur, a cough is often unproductive or may result in a small amount of clear foam. This behavior is frequently associated with feline asthma, bronchitis, or respiratory infections.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a cat coughing is Medium. While a single hacking episode may not be an immediate crisis, it is not normal for a cat to cough regularly. If the coughing is frequent, it can indicate chronic inflammation or underlying heart and lung issues. However, if your cat has a blue or purple tint to their tongue, is breathing with an open mouth, or seems to be gasping for air, this moves to an Emergency level and requires immediate veterinary intervention.

Why a Video or Photo Helps Triage

It is very common for cats to stop coughing the moment they enter a veterinary clinic due to the stress of travel. Capturing a video of the coughing episode on your phone is one of the most helpful tools for your vet. It allows the professional to see the cat's posture, hear the specific sound of the cough, and determine if the issue is upper respiratory, lower respiratory, or digestive in nature, leading to a much faster diagnosis.

faqs.question: How can I tell if it is a cough or a hairball?
faqs.answer: A hairball episode ends with the cat vomiting a cylindrical mass of fur. A cough usually involves the cat stretching their neck out low to the ground and ends with a swallowing motion or nothing at all.

faqs.question: What are common causes of coughing in cats?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include feline asthma, allergic bronchitis, lungworms, or in some cases, heartworm disease or pneumonia.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human cough medicine?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give a cat human medications. Many over-the-counter cough suppressants contain ingredients that are toxic to felines.
